input.actions_taken: Stopped the load generator container sreft-cpu-load. Recreated sreft-regex-svc with WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1 (same mitigation already used by sreft-cpu-payments), preserving network aliases, port publish, cpus=1, and restart policy. Verified /check returns 200 quickly with {"allowed":true,"rule":"disabled"} and CPU usage returned to idle. Removed the old vulnerable container.
input.postmortem:
Blameless postmortem: A production WAF path used a regex vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking. Under concurrent adversarial (or accidental) input from a load client, all workers blocked on ReDoS, so the process stayed up but stopped serving. Detection signals were high CPU with low memory, multi-second WAF warnings, and request timeouts while the process still ran.

What went well: Logs explicitly flagged catastrophic backtracking; a sibling container already documented the safe config (WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1).

Improvements: (1) Fix or replace the WAF regex and add ReDoS-safe matching (timeouts, atomic groups, or a non-regex engine). (2) Default WAF_RULE_DISABLED or a safe rule in non-prod and gate enablement behind tests. (3) Add request timeouts / worker isolation so one bad pattern cannot pin all workers. (4) Alert on sustained high CPU + elevated request latency for regex-svc. (5) Keep load generators out of production-like stacks or behind a kill switch.
input.root_cause: regex-svc was stuck at ~100% CPU and failing health/user requests because its enabled WAF regex rule suffered catastrophic backtracking (ReDoS). A load generator (sreft-cpu-load) continuously POSTed crafted ~26–27 byte payloads to /check, so worker threads spent ~21s+ per evaluation (logged as “possible catastrophic backtracking”), exhausting the worker pool and making the process appear hung while still running.

output: submission received
